statusLine: Americas crude markets remain tight with WTI at $95.43 amid significant inventory drawdowns and elevated Middle East shipping risks constraining global supply routes.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets are showing clear signs of tightening supply conditions, with commercial crude inventories falling 7.1 million barrels in the latest EIA weekly report for the week ending April 24. At 397.9 million barrels, stocks are drawing down across all major product categories, including gasoline (-6.1 MB) and distillates (-4.5 MB). US production remains robust at 13.6 million barrels per day, but the inventory decline suggests demand is outpacing domestic supply additions.

WTI crude gained 1.69% week-on-week to $95.43 per barrel in the week to April 24, though it continues trading at a significant $11.39 discount to Brent crude. This wide spread reflects ongoing global supply constraints that are keeping international prices elevated while Americas crude remains relatively abundant. The premium suggests European and Asian buyers are competing aggressively for non-Americas barrels due to Middle East shipping disruptions.

Critical maritime advisories highlight severe risks to global oil flows, with Houthi attacks in the Red Sea and Iranian threats in the Strait of Hormuz forcing tankers onto longer routes around Africa. These disruptions are not directly impacting Americas-to-Americas flows but are tightening global crude availability, supporting higher prices. The Panama Canal remains operational without major advisories, preserving the key Americas shipping artery.

Retail fuel prices continue climbing sharply, with gasoline up 32.4 cents to $4.58 per gallon and diesel rising 28.9 cents to $5.64. These increases reflect both the crude price gains and ongoing refining margin pressures as product inventories decline. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve remains at 78.8 million barrels, providing limited buffer capacity given current market tightness.
keyPoints: US crude inventories fell 7.1 million barrels with broad drawdowns across gasoline (-6.1 MB) and distillates (-4.5 MB)
WTI gained 1.69% to $95.43 in the week to April 24, but trades $11.39 below Brent due to Middle East supply disruptions
Critical maritime advisories for Red Sea and Persian Gulf shipping routes are constraining global crude flows
US retail gasoline prices surged 32.4 cents to $4.58/gal with diesel up 28.9 cents to $5.64/gal
US production steady at 13.6 million bpd but inventory declines signal demand exceeding supply additions
Strategic Petroleum Reserve at 78.8 million barrels provides limited emergency buffer in current tight market
